explanation: The property states that once the controller enters failSafeMode, it must stay in there globally (for all future times), regardless of what else happens, until a power cycle (off then on) occurs—which is equivalent to saying the controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode without a full power cycle. In other words, if failSafeMode is ever true, it remains true globally (G), which ensures no transition away from fail-safe mode is possible except by a power cycle (which, by definition, de-energizes the system and loses all controller state, so the LTL formula is scoped to the running period). This directly implements the informally stated requirement.

This property states that globally, whenever the controller is in fail-safe mode, it must continue to stay in fail-safe mode (i.e., cannot transition to anything else), until it is no longer in fail-safe mode, which can only happen if the system first goes to 'off' (fully powered down). Thus, if failSafeMode ever becomes false, it must only be right after 'off' has become true since the previous point in time failSafeMode was exited. This formalizes the idea that you can only leave fail-safe mode (i.e., failSafeMode false) after a power cycle ('off'). The template ensures that failSafeMode cannot become false unless off was true immediately prior (i.e., the only way to exit failSafeMode is via off).

The natural language states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode), it cannot exit this mode to any other unless there is a power cycle (which we must represent as 'off', i.e., the machine is turned off). The most restrictive and direct way to represent this is to specify that whenever failSafeMode is active, it remains active until 'off' (the machine has been turned off) occurs. Thus, after entering failSafeMode, it must persist (not leave) until 'off' happens. This is best captured by an invariant stating that whenever 'failSafeMode' is true, it remains true globally (i.e., cannot revert), or more generally as a safety property: 'failSafeMode' cannot become false unless 'off' occurred in between. The LTL formula G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)) (or equivalently, 'once in failSafeMode, stay there until off') would be ideal. In template terms, this is best matched to template 38 (G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))), with bool_exp1 representing 'not off' (i.e., we're not in the power-down state), and bool_exp3 representing 'not failSafeMode'. This ensures that as long as the system is not off, once failSafeMode is active, it stays active. However, template 8 (G (! (condition))) can encode directly that globally it is not the case that the system is in a state where it has left failSafeMode (e.g., left failSafeMode without going through off).

To be as direct as possible, I select template 8 (G (! bool_exp3)) with bool_exp3 = exitFromFailSafeModeWithoutOff, where exitFromFailSafeModeWithoutOff is defined as ( (!failSafeMode) & (Y (failSafeMode & (! off))) ). This means: it is not possible to exit failSafeMode (failSafeMode becomes false) immediately after a state where the system was in failSafeMode but was not off.

Since our templates do not directly support this degree of expressiveness, the next best is to use template 8: G (! ( (!failSafeMode) & (!off) ) ), meaning 'never is there a state where the system is not in failSafeMode and is not off' – i.e., if we're not off, we must always be in failSafeMode if we entered it. This is correct since after entering failSafeMode, only off can break this requirement.

The property asserts that once the system is in fail-safe mode (failSafeMode), it must remain there (always failSafeMode) until the controller is power-cycled off (off is true). In other words, returning to any other mode (leaving fail-safe mode) is not possible without turning off the machine. This uses 'failSafeMode' to indicate the controller is in fail-safe mode, and 'off' to indicate the system has been power-cycled. The response template chosen requires that globally, if 'failSafeMode' holds, then it will continue to hold until 'off' is true (a power cycle occurs), matching the intent in the natural language description.

The requirement states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it must not return to any other mode (i.e., must remain in failSafeMode) until the machine is power cycled (turned off and then on again). This can be expressed by stating that globally, once failSafeMode becomes true, it stays true until off becomes true (indicating the machine has powered off). Here, 'failSafeMode' means the fail-safe mode is active, and 'off' means the system is completely de-energized. This is most simply and strictly expressed as: globally, if failSafeMode is true, then failSafeMode must stay true at all future steps until off.

Thus, 'G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off))' captures the requirement, but since the template set uses a slightly different style, the closest match is 'G (bool_exp1 -> (G (! bool_exp3)))', but we can more accurately use 'G (failSafeMode -> (G failSafeMode))' until 'off' occurs, but as the template set has only a template for 'always not something', the closest direct encoding is template 17: 'G (bool_exp1 -> (G bool_exp3))', with bool_exp1 = failSafeMode, bool_exp3 = failSafeMode. This enforces that once failSafeMode is true, it remains true forever (unless power is cycled; at which point the controller can be restarted and the guarantee re-applies).

The requirement is that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot leave this mode except by a power cycle (turn OFF, then ON). Based on the atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' flags that the controller is in that state, and 'off' means the system is fully powered down. To capture this requirement, we want to express that it is always the case (globally) that if the controller is in fail-safe mode, it must remain in fail-safe mode until it is turned off. In LTL, this is best described using the 'until' operator: (failSafeMode) U off, but to enforce that any transition from failSafeMode can only go to off directly (i.e., the system must turn off before leaving fail-safe mode), we must ensure that whenever failSafeMode is true, it remains true until off holds. This is expressed as: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). However, using only boolean expressions as required, we select the template 'G (failSafeMode -> (G (failSafeMode | off)))', but this permits 'off' to be true concurrently with leaving 'failSafeMode', which matches the semantics. Thus, globally, once failSafeMode is entered, either failSafeMode remains true or off becomes true, but the system cannot transition out of failSafeMode without being off. This is a strong approximation of the NL constraint.

The chosen template (ID 18) expresses that once the system is in fail-safe mode (failSafeMode), it can never leave fail-safe mode unless the system is turned off (off). This matches the requirement that the controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode without a power cycle. Here, G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)) ensures that, from the time failSafeMode is entered, it must remain true until off (i.e., a power cycle) occurs, which resets the mode.

The requirement states that once the system enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes true), it cannot leave fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes false) unless a power cycle occurs (the machine is turned off and then turned on). The only allowed escape from fail-safe mode is through a power cycle. If we consider that 'off' indicates the machine is powered off, then the only valid way to get from failSafeMode active to any non-failSafeMode state is that the machine first turns off before failSafeMode can be deactivated. The property can be captured by stating it is always the case that if the system leaves fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes false at the next step), then it must be the case that previously (right now) the system was off, i.e., transitioning out from failSafeMode requires 'off' to be true in the current step. In LTL, a typical encoding is that it is always the case that if currently we are in failSafeMode and at the next step we are not in failSafeMode, then in the current or previous step, the system must have been off. However, with the atomic props at hand, a strict direct encoding is: G ((failSafeMode & (X (!failSafeMode))) -> (X off)). This states that a transition from failSafeMode to NOT failSafeMode can only happen if, in the next step, the system is off (i.e., only possible through a power cycle).

The requirement is that once the controller enters 'failSafeMode', it cannot return to any other mode except if there is a power cycle (system turns off and on again). To express that while 'failSafeMode' is true, it cannot be false unless the system is 'off' (powers down), we use the template G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode W off)). This property says globally: if failSafeMode is active, it will continue to be active until off becomes active (i.e., until the system is turned off). In this template, 'failSafeMode' is bool_exp1 and '(failSafeMode W off)' is a temporal formula, but for the template, we treat 'failSafeMode' as our enabling condition and 'failSafeMode' as the state that must persist until 'off' happens. Therefore, template 37: G (bool_exp1 -> (G bool_exp3)), with bool_exp1 = failSafeMode and bool_exp3 = (failSafeMode | off), states: "Whenever failSafeMode is true, always (from that point onward) either failSafeMode is true or off is true." This ensures that after entering failSafeMode we cannot return to a normal operation mode until complete power off.

The property expresses that once the controller enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes true), it cannot transition out of fail-safe mode (i.e., "not in failSafeMode") until a power cycle occurs (the system turns off and then on). This is formalized by stating that globally, if the system ever leaves fail-safe mode (i.e., failSafeMode becomes false in the next state after being true), this is only possible if the system went through an off state (off=true) in the meantime. The chosen template guarantees that exiting fail-safe can occur only via a power cycle, as described in the natural language requirement.
